content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Steven A. Herum 
Attorney-at-Law 
Herum Crabtree Suntag 
5757 Pacific Avenue, Suite 222 
Stockton, CA 95207 
May 23, 2017 
Re: 
Your Request for Infonnal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-17-099 
Dear Mr. Herum: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of an unnamed public official 
regarding the gift/honoraria provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 As we noted in our 
prior correspondence of April 17, 2017, Regulation 18329(b)(2) states: 
"Requests for fonnal written advice will not be acted upon unless the 
following requirements arc met: 
"(A) The name, title or position, and mailing address of the person whose 
duties are in question arc provided. In addition, if the request is submitted by an 
authorized representative, it shall contain a specific statement that such 
authorization has been made. 
"(B) All the facts material to the consideration of the question or questions 
presented have been provided in a clear and concise manner." 
Given that you have not provided the name of the official on whose behalf you request the 
advice, we treat your request as one for infonnal assistance. lnfonnal assistance does not provide 
the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal written advice. (Section 83114; 
Regulation 18329( c)(3 ).) 
Also, note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including common law conflicts 
of interest or the doctrine of incompatible activities which may also impact your client's 
participation in such activities. 
1 The Political Reform Act 1s con tamed in Government Code Section.,; 81000 through 9101-t All statutory 
references are to the GO\emment Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. 1-17-099 
Page No. 2 
Your office represents a client who is also a full time elected official. The public agency that 
the client serves does not have direct influence or regulatory control over educational programs or 
policies. 
Previously the client served as a part time public official and was employed as a 
schoolteacher and educational consultant. The client also has an expertise in educational policy and 
holds a highly aspirational personal biography that starts with growing up in a challenging 
neighborhood with a single mother and culminating in receiving two degrees from a prestigious 
private university. 
In order to be compensated for speaking at educational conferences and forums the client 
proposes that the professional speaking contract with the organization sponsoring the event and 
paying the speakers contain the following provisions: 
• The client's presentation shall be limited to educational matters and he/she shall not address 
nor answer questions of political nature not directly related to educational policy or 
programs; 
• The client shall decline lo answer any questions or participate in any discussions relating to 
his/her elected position or policy issues facing the public agency he/she represents; 
• He/she shall limit the speech or presentation and any written material to educational matters 
only, shall not be introduced as an elected official during the speaking engagement or 
referred to as an elected official in any advertising materials prior to the speaking 
engagement, and will decline questions or participating in any discussions relating to the 
elected position; and 
• He/she shall automatically without providing the organizing entity any written notice recuse 
himself/herself from any and all government business. Decisions or actions by the public 
agency he/she represents that relate to the event sponsor or the entity directly or indirectly 
paying compensation to the speakers at the event. 
You stated that the limiting provisions are intended to give assurance that this is a legitimate 
educational speaking function and not a disguised honorarium. You also stated that the public 
official's automatic recusal from participating in any government decision involving the event 
sponsor is intended to demonstrate that the payment is not a disguised political contribution. 
General Conflicts of111tercst 
. 
. 
ANALYSIS 
You asked whether your client may receive compensation for speaking on educational 
policy or programs, in contrast to political or other governmental topics, at conventions and other 
events. As an initial matter, be aware that the Act prohibits an official from making, participating in 
making or using his or her official position to influence a government decision in which the official 
has a financial interest, such as a source of income of SS00 or more. (Section 87100.) 


File No. 1-17-099 
Page No. 3 
Section 87103 of the Act provides that an official has a financial interest in a decision within 
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the source of income. 
You noted that: 
• The client shall automatically without providing the organizing entity any written notice 
recuse himself/herself from all government business, decisions or actions by the public 
agency the client represents that relate to the event sponsor or the entity directly or 
indirectly paying compensation to the speakers at the event. 
So long as the client docs not make, participate in making, or influence a governmental 
decision that will foresecably and materially affect a source of income, the client will not be 
violating the conflict of interest provisions of the Act. 
Honoraria! 
Section 89503(a} prohibits a designated employee of a state agency from accepting 
honoraria from any source, if the official would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts 
from that source on his or her statement of economic interest. "Honorarium" means ... any 
payment made in consideration for any speech given, article published, or attendance at any public 
or private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like gathering. (Section 
89502(b).) You noted: 
• The client's presentation shall be limited to educational matters and he/she shall not address 
nor answer questions of political nature not directly related to educational policy or 
programs. 
• The client shall decline to answer any questions or participate in any discussions relating to 
his/her elected position or policy issues facing the public agency he/she represents. 
• 
He/she shall limit the speech or presentation and any written material to educational matters 
only and shall not be introduced as an elected official during the speaking engagement or 
referred to as an elected official in any advertising materials prior to the speaking 
engagement and will decline answering questions or participating in any discussions relating 
to the elected position. 
However. under the current statute, state elected officers are prohibited from accepting an 
honorarium for any speech given, regardless of the co11te111 of tlw speech. In 1990, in the Watson 
Advice Letter, No. I-90-742. we stated: 
"The former statute relating to the limitations on gifts and honorarium, 
Section 85400. had placed a S 1,000 limit on any speech relating to the 
? Generally. the gift and contribution pro\'is1ons are not 1mphcated by the receipt of .. speaking fees" since gifts 
and contributions arc defined to not include payments for \\'hich full consideration \\ as prO\ ided. (Sec Section 82028 
and Section 82015.) 


File No. l-17-099 
Page No. 4 
governmental process. This statute was repealed and replaced by Sections 8950 I 
- 89506 .... Under the current statute, state elected officers are prohibited from 
accepting an honorarium for any speech given, regardless of the content of the 
speech." 
The statute was amended again in 1996 to apply the same rule prohibiting state officials 
from accepting an honorarium for any speech given, regardless of the content of the speech, to 
local public officials. The content-based S 1,000 limit was repealed. 
Thus, while the precautionary actions may be prudent to prevent violations of an 
agency's statement against incompatibility activities, they would not impact the application of 
the honoraria prohibition. Thus, absent an exception, your client's activity as a paid speaker is an 
activity covered by the honoraria prohibitions and the funds the client would receive are 
payments made in consideration for the "speech given" would be prohibited. 
Stat11t01y and Reg11/a101:1' Exceptions 
1. Earned Income 
Section 89501 (b)( I) provides that the term "honorarium" does not include: 
"Earned income for personal services which are customarily provided in 
connection with the practice of a bona fide business, trade, or profession, such as 
teaching, practicing law, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or building 
contracting, unless the sole or predominant activity of the business, trade, or 
profession is making speeches. The Commission shall adopt regulations to 
implement this subdivision." 
Interpreting Section 8950 I (b )(I), Regulation I 8932(a) provides as follows: 
"(a) 'Honorarium' docs not include income earned from personal services if: 
"( 1) The services arc provided in connection with an individual's business 
or the individual's practice of or employment in a bona fide business, trade, or 
profession, such as teaching, practicing law. medicine, insurance, real estate, 
banking, or building contracting, pursuant to [Regulations 18932.1 through 
18932.3]; and 
"(2) The services arc customarily provided in connection with the 
business, trade, or profession.'' 
Thus, personal services provided by an individual in connection with that individual's bona 
fide business are not included within the Act's definition ofan honorarium. Regulation 18932.l (b) 
sets forth the test for determining when an individual's business that has been in existence for less 
than two calendar years is "bona fide" and provides as follows: 


File No. l-17-099 
Page No. 5 
"(b) For purposes of Government Code Sections 8950 I through 89506, a 
business that has been in existence for less than two calendar years is presumed to 
be "bona fide" if: 
"( 1) Its owner has maintained records of income and expenses, consistent 
with the operation of a business, from the date of inception of the claimed 
business; and, 
"(2) Any three of the following are maintained: 
"(A) Copies of tax returns filed in connection with the operation of the 
claimed business for one year; 
"(8) Records of bank accounts or lines of credit in the name of the 
business; 
"(C) Records of purchase, lease, or rental of equipment and supplies for 
use solely by the business; 
"(D) Records of expenditures for rent, wages, business or professional 
insurance, or other similar, customary costs of doing business; 
"(E) Records of efforts to market good or services provided by the 
business; or 
"(F) Documentation of expertise by the business operator in connection 
with the service provided or product sold by the business." 
A business that has been in business for less than two years is "bona fide" if the official has 
maintained records of income and expenses consistent with the operation of a business since the 
inception of the business and if you have maintained specified other records. 
Despite meeting the requirements of Regulation 18932.1 (b ), a business is not a "bona fide 
business" if speechmaking is its sole or predominant activity. Regulation I 8932.3(a) provides that 
the "predominant activity" of a business in existence for one year or more is presumed to be 
speechmaking if during the 12-month period prior to and including the date of the speech, more 
than SO percent of the hours spent on the business is devoted to, or more than 50 percent of the 
brross income of the business is derived from, the preparation and/or delivery of speeches. 
2. Direct Charitable Donations (Regulation 18931.5.) 
A charitable donation made in lieu of a speaking fee is not an honorarium if: 
( 1) The donation is made directly to a bona fide charitable, educational, civic, religious, or 
similar tax-exempt, nonprofit organization, and is not delivered to the individual: 


File No. 1-17-099 
Page No. 6 
(2) The speaker does not make the donation a condition for his or her speech, article, or 
attendance; 
(3) The speaker does not claim the donation as a deduction from income for tax purposes; 
( 4) The donation will have no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the speaker, or on 
any member of the speaker's immediate family; and 
(5) The speaker is not identified to the recipient organization in connection with the 
donation. If the speaker knows or has reason to know that a donor intends to make such a donation, . 
the speaker must inform the donor that the donation cannot be made in the speaker's name. 
3. Rct11rni11g flonoraria (Reg11latio11 18933.) 
(a) The prohibitions imposed on acceptance of an honorarium by Section 89502 do not 
apply if, within 30 days of receipt of an honorarium, any of the following occurs: 
(I) The speaker returns the honorarium, unused, to the source of the payment or the source's 
agent or intennediary, if any; 
(2) The speaker delivers the honorarium to the Controller for donation to the General Fund 
(in the case of state officials or candidates), or to the speaker's agency for donation to an equivalent 
fund (in the case of local officials or candidates), and does not claim the donation as a deduction for 
income tax purposes; or 
(3) If the honorarium is not a payment of money and if the speaker is unable to comply with 
subdivision (a)( I) or subdivision (a)(2) above, the speaker reimburses the donor or the donor's 
agent or intcnnediary, if any, for the valǢc or use of the honorarium. 
(b) Neither the speaker nor the donor shall be required to report any honorarium returned, 
deli\'ered or reimbursed pursuant to subdi\'ision (a) as an honorarium, income. gift, or activity 
cxpcm,e. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
JWW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
John 
\ Wallace 
Assistant General Counsel 
Legal 
1ivision

sections.question: Whether a full-time elected public official may receive compensation for speaking on educational policy or programs at conventions and other events, given that the official's public agency does not have direct influence or regulatory control over educational programs or policies, and given proposed contractual provisions limiting the speech to educational matters, declining to address political topics or questions about the official's elected position, not being introduced as an elected official, and automatically recusing from government decisions relating to the event sponsor.
sections.conclusion: The document concludes that a local public official is prohibited from accepting an honorarium for any speech given, regardless of content. However, the earned income exception under Section 89501(b)(1) may apply if the official has established a bona fide educational consulting business (in existence for 2+ years or meeting specific documentation requirements) where speaking is customary but not the sole or predominant activity. The official must maintain contemporaneous records documenting that speaking represents no more than 50% of business activity. Additionally, the official must avoid conflicts of interest by refraining from making or influencing governmental decisions with material financial effects on the speaking income source.

General Conflicts of111tercst

"(b) For purposes of Government Code Sections 8950 I through 89506, a

sections.question_synthetic: May a public official receive compensation for educational speaking engagements where the speech content is limited to educational policy and the official recuses from related governmental decisions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Local public officials are generally prohibited from accepting honoraria for speeches regardless of content, but may receive earned income from speaking if it qualifies as part of a legitimate bona fide business (where speaking is not the sole or predominant activity) and provided they maintain required documentation and avoid conflicts of interest through proper recusal.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an FPPC response to an informal advice request (requestor did not provide official's name as required for formal advice). Document contains comprehensive FACTS, ANALYSIS sections with CONCLUSION embedded in analytical discussion. Conclusion is synthesized from multiple sections discussing honoraria prohibitions and earned income exceptions. Citation to Watson Advice Letter and detailed regulatory framework provided.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a full-time elected public official may receive compensation for speaking on educational policy or programs at conventions and other events, given that the official's public agency does not have direct influence or regulatory control over educational programs or policies, and given proposed contractual provisions limiting the speech to educational matters, declining to address political topics or questions about the official's elected position, not being introduced as an elected official, and automatically recusing from government decisions relating to the event sponsor.

CONCLUSION: The document concludes that a local public official is prohibited from accepting an honorarium for any speech given, regardless of content. However, the earned income exception under Section 89501(b)(1) may apply if the official has established a bona fide educational consulting business (in existence for 2+ years or meeting specific documentation requirements) where speaking is customary but not the sole or predominant activity. The official must maintain contemporaneous records documenting that speaking represents no more than 50% of business activity. Additionally, the official must avoid conflicts of interest by refraining from making or influencing governmental decisions with material financial effects on the speaking income source.

embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter 17-099 addresses whether a full-time local elected official may be compensated for educational speaking engagements. The FPPC concludes that while the honoraria prohibition generally applies to all speeches, an earned income exception may apply if the speaking is part of an established bona fide educational consulting or teaching business where speaking is customary but not predominant, and provided the official maintains business records and recuses from related governmental decisions.
